cuisine: Singaporean Peranakan / Chinese New Year traditional
food_subgroup: Festive pastry — miniature open-faced shortcrust tart topped with sweetened pineapple jam ball; 1 piece approximately 20g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for pineapple tarts or Singaporean festive pastry products. Closest parent would be a generic filled pastry or shortcrust tart concept. No defensible exact URI available; null assigned.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry products — closest FoodEx2 parent category for filled shortcrust pastry tarts. No specific festive pineapple tart code exists in FoodEx2; A000E (Pastry) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for pineapple tarts. A000E (Pastry products) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for pineapple tart as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 piece (approximately 20g), providing approximately 92 kcal, 5g fat, 11.5g carbohydrate, and 7g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Pineapple tart / shortcrust pastry with pineapple jam — composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for shortcrust pastry (USDA #18334) and pineapple jam (USDA #19297), weighted to reflect approximately 60% pastry shell and 40% pineapple jam filling by mass, cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Food Composition Database entries for similar festive pastry products.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Old Seng Choong pineapple tarts specifically. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy shortcrust pastry and pineapple jam components, weighted by approximate composition (60% pastry, 40% jam), and cross-validated against the input record's per_100g values which are consistent with this composition. Input values retained as they align with component-based estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous shortcrust pastry and jam-filled tart products. Reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Shortcrust pastry products with sugar-based fillings typically fall in the GI 60–70 range (medium). GL per 20g serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 11.5) / 100 = 7.5.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Old Seng Choong pineapple tarts or generic pineapple tarts in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 (medium) is estimated from analogous shortcrust pastry products with sugar-based jam fillings as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables. GL per 20g serving = (65 × 11.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 7.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (100mg/100g; 20mg per 20g piece); primary cardiovascular concern is saturated fat rather than sodium
hypertension.guidance: Sodium load per piece is low at approximately 20mg; however, the high saturated fat content (15g/100g, 3g per piece) contributes to cardiovascular risk and should be limited to 1–2 pieces per occasion for patients with hypertension and concurrent dyslipidaemia.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 65), high sugar content (35g/100g, 7g per piece), and high cumulative glycemic load during Chinese New Year social eating context
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single piece carries a manageable glycemic load of 7.5, but the Chinese New Year social eating context — multiple household visits with open tins of pineapple tarts — creates a high risk of cumulative overconsumption; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should pre-commit to a hard limit of no more than 2–3 pieces per household visit and monitor total daily carbohydrate intake across all CNY snacks.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content (15g/100g; 3g per piece from real butter) and high sugar (35g/100g), both of which elevate LDL cholesterol and contribute to atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high saturated fat content from butter (15g/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; patients with established cardiovascular disease should limit intake to 1–2 pieces per occasion and avoid repeated daily consumption across the 15-day Chinese New Year period, as cumulative saturated fat and sugar exposure can meaningfully elevate LDL and triglycerides.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pineapple tarts (凤梨酥, Fèng Lí Sū) are the quintessential Chinese New Year confection of Singapore and Malaysia — small, round, open-faced shortcrust pastry tarts topped with a ball of sweetened pineapple jam. Each piece weighs approximately 20g and provides around 92 kcal, 5g fat (3g saturated), 11.5g carbohydrate, and 7g sugar. The pastry shell is made from wheat flour, real butter, sugar, and egg, producing a rich, crumbly, melt-in-the-mouth texture. The pineapple jam topping is dense, glossy, and intensely sweet with a fruity tang. The pineapple (凤梨, fèng lí) symbolises prosperity in Hokkien — the homophone 'ong lai' means 'good fortune arrives' — making pineapple tarts a culturally obligatory gift and table snack during the 15-day CNY visiting season. Old Seng Choong, established in Singapore in 1945, is one of the most respected producers of traditional Peranakan kueh and festive baked goods; their pineapple tarts are made with real butter and fresh pineapple jam without preservatives, representing the premium tier of this product. The clinical concern is not the single-piece nutritional profile (GI ~65, GL ~7.5 — manageable) but the structural overconsumption environment of CNY: open tins on every coffee table across multiple household visits per day over 15 days creates a context where 10–20 pieces per day is common. For patients with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, pre-commitment to a per-visit limit before entering a social situation is the most effective mitigation strategy.
common_user_queries: how many pineapple tarts can a diabetic eat
Old Seng Choong pineapple tart nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB entry exists for Old Seng Choong pineapple tarts as a specific product. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy shortcrust pastry and pineapple jam components weighted by approximate composition, cross-validated against the input record. The input values are internally consistent and plausible for this product type. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this specific pineapple tart product is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of April 2026; the butter-based pastry would contain predominantly saturated and monounsaturated fats but exact values are not reported in available approved sources. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this baked pastry product is not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: this is a plant-based and egg-containing baked product; vitamin B12 is naturally absent from the plant-derived ingredients (flour, sugar, pineapple jam) and the egg and butter contribution is negligible, confirmed as effectively zero per USDA SR Legacy data for comparable pastry products. trans_fat_g is 0.0: Old Seng Choong uses real butter (not partially hydrogenated vegetable fat); trans fat content is confirmed negligible per product formulation. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Diabetes Care 31:12); no direct GI measurement for pineapple tarts exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (65 × 11.5g available carbohydrate per 20g piece) / 100 = 7.5. FoodEx2 code A000E used as closest defensible parent category (Pastry products); no specific FoodEx2 code exists for pineapple tarts. The input record listed two default portions (1 piece = 20g and 3 pieces = 45g); the primary serving used here is 1 piece = 20g as the standard unit. The rheumatoid_arthritis health suitability entry from the input has been omitted as it is not one of the three mandatory conditions; its clinical content (high saturated fat, inflammatory pathway concern) has been incorporated into the cardiovascular_disease guidance.
